turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to use a custom allocator in my Vec but the compiler keeps complaining about missing traits. It feels like I should just be able to pass an object like I do with regular memory, not have to manually implement these weird trait bounds.

That feeling is super common when you first dive into the new Allocator API on nightly. Think of it like this: in standard Rust, a Vec just needs one allocator for its entire lifetime, but now with per-collection allocators, you're basically giving each collection its own dedicated memory manager so they can behave differently without sharing state.
So like if I want my data structures to use a bumpalo-backed arena instead of the global heap? But how do I actually attach that allocator to a Vec without breaking type safety?

Exactly, and here's where it gets interesting. You don't just pass an allocator object directly like in C or Go; instead you implement the Allocator trait for your custom allocator so that the compiler knows how to handle memory requests from that specific type.
Right, so I need to derive or manually impl the Allocator trait? And then what exactly do those methods actually take as arguments?

You typically implement it manually for more control. The key method is allocate which takes a capacity hint and returns a pointer, while free handles deallocation. These aren't just regular functions; they're part of the trait system that lets you plugging your allocator into Vec<T, A> where A is your custom type.
Okay so if I'm building a small arena allocator similar to bumpalo but with my own allocation strategy, do I just need to expose those two methods?

Not quite. You definitely need those core methods for basic allocation and deallocation logic, but since you're on nightly, there's also a more ergonomic way using the allocator-api crate which provides helper traits that make implementing Allocator much less verbose.
Oh wow, I didn't know there was an entire ecosystem built around this. Does it integrate cleanly with bumpalo or do I have to rewrite everything?

You don't need to rewrite everything; you can layer your allocator on top of bumpalo's existing infrastructure. The Allocator trait acts like a bridge so that even if you're using bumpalo internally, your custom Vec<T, A> still gets the right memory safety guarantees without exposing its internals.
So basically I can create my own allocator that wraps bumpalo and then just use it with collections like HashMap or BTreeSet?
Yes, exactly. Collections like HashMap or BTreeSet now support per-collection allocators too, so you can pass your custom allocator to them independently of the rest of your program.
That's pretty neat. Does this mean I could have some parts of my app using a zero-copy allocator and others using bumpalo without any performance penalty?
Perfectly, that's the whole point. By decoupling allocation from collection ownership, you get fine-grained control over memory usage patterns across your entire application.
One last thing though, what happens if I accidentally forget to impl the Allocator trait properly and my code compiles but runs into a panic later?

The compiler will catch most of it at build time with clear error messages about missing trait implementations. If you somehow slip through, runtime panics are rare because the trait bounds act as a hard guard against invalid memory operations.

I'm trying to build a tool that caches thousands of small objects in memory, but whenever I try to use std::collections::HashMap with an arena allocator it just refuses to compile. Why does this even work this way? I expected the allocator to pass through like magic.

That frustration is actually quite common when stepping into the Allocator trait on nightly because Rust's type system treats allocators as part of the collection's identity rather than a transparent parameter. The HashMap implementation doesn't expose a generic constructor that accepts an allocator argument by default, which means you can't just pass your Arena to it and expect it to use that memory directly without changing how you define the map itself.

So if I want to store objects in a single arena using bumpalo, do I have to replace HashMap entirely? Or is there a way to make Vec<T> work with an allocator so I can just push items into it and they'll all live on that same heap region?

You don't necessarily need to replace HashMap if you only care about the allocation behavior, but for true per-collection control without changing the API surface too much, you should look at using Vec<T> with a generic allocator parameter or implementing your own collection wrapper. The key insight is that on nightly, the Allocator trait allows you to pass an Arena directly into collections like Vec, so instead of standardizing on one global allocator for everything, you can create specialized versions where each container gets its own memory pool from your Bumpalo instance.

That sounds promising but I'm worried about lifetime and ownership complications. If I give a collection an arena that's being dropped later, will the data still be accessible? And how do I make sure my custom collections actually implement Serialize if they use this new allocator pattern?

Lifetime is handled automatically because the allocator itself owns the memory region, so as long as you keep the Arena alive in scope, all objects allocated through that specific collection remain valid. Regarding serialization, since your collections now hold references to arbitrary types backed by an arena rather than raw pointers or standard heap locations, they typically won't implement Serialize out of the box unless you provide custom serializers that track which arena region each object belongs to.
Okay so I need a custom serializer? Is there a standard trait for this on nightly, and does it integrate well with serde_json::Value? Or do I have to roll my own serialization logic from scratch?

There isn't a built-in trait specifically for arena-backed collections yet in the stable ecosystem, so you'll likely need to implement custom logic that wraps your allocator-based collection and exposes its contents via an iterator or by tracking offsets within the Bumpalo buffer. You can then manually construct serde_json::Value structures from those iterators, effectively bypassing the standard serialization path since these collections don't conform to normal heap memory assumptions.

Got it, but I'm still stuck on how to actually define a Vec<T> that takes an allocator parameter instead of just using the default one. Do I need to write a full newtype wrapper or is there a macro I can use?

You will almost certainly need to write a simple newtype wrapper struct around your collection type since no blanket impl exists for arbitrary allocators in Vec yet on stable. This allows you to define a custom allocator-aware variant like MyVec<T, A> that implements the necessary traits and passes the Allocator through to the underlying storage mechanism.

Understood, so I'll create a wrapper around Vec with an explicit allocator field. One last question: does this approach conflict with any existing crates that assume standard heap allocation, or will they break if I start using these per-allocator collections?

Your custom allocator-based collections won't break most existing crates as long as you don't pass them into functions expecting standard heap pointers. They'll only interact differently with code that explicitly uses the Allocator trait or relies on stable Vec behavior, so your tool should remain compatible unless you're integrating deeply with legacy systems.

Why does this even work this way? I just tried using a custom allocator in my Vec and it felt like magic, but the documentation for the new nightly features is confusing. It seems like we can now pass an arena allocator directly to collections instead of just having a global one.

You're seeing the shift from a single global allocator to a per-collection allocator model via the Allocator trait on nightly. This means you create specialized allocators like Arena or Bumpalo for specific data structures rather than relying on the default System allocator everywhere. The Vec<T, A> generic now accepts an allocator type parameter so your collections can use a dedicated memory pool instead of the heap.

That sounds good but I'm still confused about how to actually implement this for my side project. If I want to allocate large chunks of data without fragmentation, do I just need to drop-in a bumpalo allocator into my Vec? Or is there some more complex setup required?

For your use case with large contiguous blocks, Bumpalo or the new Arena allocator from the allocator-api crate would be perfect since they manage memory in a fixed-size arena rather than growing the heap. You don't need complex setup beyond defining an impl for your custom type if you want to extend it further. The key is using the Allocator trait bound on Vec so you can swap in different allocators based on performance requirements.

Okay, I think I understand the general idea but what about when my data structure needs to track ownership of memory across multiple allocations? The Allocator trait seems like it should handle this better than just passing a Vec<T> around.

Exactly, that's why the new systems approach allows you to implement custom logic for tracking ownership. You can create your own allocator struct that implements the Allocator trait and manages its internal state like a bumpalo arena or a custom slab. This lets you track which memory blocks belong to which logical unit of data without needing manual free calls.
So if I'm building a new allocator for my project, do I need to worry about implementing all the trait methods like alloc, dealloc, and init? Or can I just use an existing crate and pass it directly?

You only need to implement those three core methods if you're creating a custom allocator from scratch. For most cases, crates like bumpalo or the new allocator-api crate provide fully implemented types that you can instantiate and pass directly into your Vec<T, A> without writing any trait logic yourself.
I see, so if I'm working on a high-performance server component where latency matters more than flexibility, which allocator would be best to use? The bumpalo one or the new arena approach?

For maximum performance with minimal overhead, the new Arena allocator from the nightly Allocator API is ideal since it avoids pointer chasing and fragmentation. Bumpalo is great too but slightly more flexible for mixed workloads. The Arena approach gives you zero-cost abstractions if your data structures are uniform.
That makes sense, so I should just replace my existing Vec<T> calls with Vec<T, A> where A is the arena allocator? And then pass that to all my collection functions?

Yes, exactly. You simply instantiate your vector with the new generic parameter and then use it throughout your codebase. The compiler will ensure that every allocation goes through your chosen allocator instead of the global system allocator.
I'm trying to understand the difference between using a single global allocator and passing a custom allocator like an arena directly to collections.

The core distinction is that you can't just use an Arena trait object with Vec<T> because it requires a concrete type. You need to pass the specific allocation implementation, like from Bumpalo or a custom struct implementing Allocator, when constructing your collections.
So if I want to use an arena allocator with Vec<T>, do I have to wrap it in some special trait object first?

Not exactly. You can't pass a generic TraitObject to Vec because the memory layout needs to be known at compile time for safety, but you can create your own allocator struct that implements Allocator and pass that concrete type directly into the collections.
That makes sense. But what about when I'm using a custom allocator with a Vec, does it actually replace the heap?

Yes, if you use an arena-based allocator like Bumpalo's implementation, the Vec will allocate from that specific memory pool instead of the standard heap. This means your collection and any iterators over it are tied to that single allocation.
Okay, so if I have a Vec<T> backed by an arena, can I still use regular drop logic?
You can, but you need to be careful with the Drop trait. If your collection holds references or handles that depend on the heap, they won't work once you switch to a custom allocator like Bumpalo.
So I should only use per-collection allocators for data structures where the memory ownership is self-contained?
Exactly. That's why this approach shines in systems programming contexts like garbage collection or stack-based simulations, but it's a bit more complex than just calling malloc.

I've been following the new nightly docs on memory management, and it feels a bit like shifting from using std::alloc to something more granular where we can specify an allocator per collection rather than just having one global default. It reminds me of how HashMap used to be tied to the heap but now we might have something similar for our collections.

That's exactly the shift happening with the Allocator trait in the nightly compiler. Instead of passing a single Alloc to every Vec, you can now pass an A into a Vec<T, A> which lets different parts of your program use distinct allocators like Bumpalo or a custom arena allocator. This means you aren't just managing raw heap blocks anymore; you're managing ownership of allocation strategies at the type level.

So if I'm building a system with multiple concurrent threads, each needing its own memory pool to avoid contention, how does that fit into this new trait? Is it like having separate arenas for different subsystems instead of one big shared allocator?

Yes, think of it as the Rust equivalent of per-thread local storage but generalized. You can create distinct Alloc instances and pass them down through your data structures so each thread or subsystem gets its own arena without needing to coordinate with a global allocator. This eliminates the contention that plagues std::alloc when multiple threads try to grab from a single pool.

I see, but what happens if I accidentally mix allocators in my code? Does the compiler catch that at compile time like it does for Send or Sync traits? Can't I just pass any Alloc and let runtime errors handle the rest?

The Allocator trait itself is a blanket trait implemented by many types, but when you actually use it with concrete allocators, the system enforces coherence through your type definitions. If you try to construct a Vec<T, A1> where A1 isn't compatible with how it was intended to be used in that context, the compiler will warn or error because the trait bounds won't align properly. It's like trying to put incompatible memory pools together; Rust forces you to define them correctly so they can coexist.

That makes sense, but I'm worried about performance overhead if every Vec<T> needs its own allocation logic instead of just using heap allocs. Is there a cost to this approach? Does it slow down our startup or runtime significantly?

There is an upfront cost in terms of compile-time complexity and some runtime overhead for tracking which allocator owns which memory block, but the trade-off is usually worth it for systems with high contention on the heap. The main benefit is reducing fragmentation because small allocations can go into a dedicated arena rather than competing for large heap chunks. This keeps your startup slightly slower due to more complex initialization but makes runtime allocation much faster and predictable.

Got it, so this changes how we think about memory layout in our programs. If I'm designing a service with many small buffers, should I be using this trait or sticking with the standard heap? What's the real use case here beyond just being cool?

The real use case is when you need deterministic memory behavior and predictable fragmentation patterns. For example, in high-frequency trading or game engines where every nanosecond counts, having a dedicated allocator for your buffer pools prevents the jitter that comes from global heap contention. You're not just moving code; you're changing how your program interacts with hardware at the lowest level.

That's a strong point about predictability. But what if I want to support both optimized paths and fallbacks? Can this trait help me build a hybrid system where some collections use bumpalo and others stick to std::alloc without breaking things?

Absolutely, the Allocator API is designed for exactly that flexibility. You can define different traits or concrete implementations depending on your context, then pass the appropriate allocator into each Vec<T, A>. This lets you have a unified interface while allowing different underlying strategies per collection. It's like having a single API with multiple implementations behind it.
I think I get how this helps with modular design now. But what about when dealing with raw pointers or unsafe code? Does the trait change anything there, or do we still need to be careful?

It doesn't remove the need for care; in fact, it makes you more explicit about where allocations happen. When you have a Vec<T, A>, any pointer you get from that vector is guaranteed to come from A's allocation strategy. This clarity helps you reason about unsafe code because you know exactly which allocator owns each block of memory.

Alright, so the key takeaway is that this trait lets us move away from monolithic heap management toward more granular control per collection. But I still feel like there might be some hidden complexity in how these allocators interact with the rest of our ecosystem. Is there anything else we should consider?

You're right to question the hidden complexity. The main thing to watch is ensuring your custom allocators are implemented correctly according to the trait bounds, especially around dropping and reusing memory. Also, be mindful that while this gives you control, it means you now have more moving parts to reason about, so testing becomes slightly more involved.

Understood. So if I were to implement a custom allocator for my team's internal data structures, what would be the first step? Do we need to write new code or can we just use existing crates like bumpalo?

You can start by integrating existing crates like bumpalo into your project and passing them as allocators where appropriate. That gives you immediate benefits without needing to write a custom allocator from scratch. If you later want full control, the trait provides the interface you need to implement your own logic.
Perfect, so I think we have enough clarity now. Let's start integrating bumpalo into our data structures for those high-contention sections and see how it performs compared to the old way.

That sounds like a solid plan. Just remember to test your custom allocations carefully since they don't always follow the same patterns as standard heap allocs, but with the right allocator in place, you should see significant improvements in performance and predictability.
Thanks for walking through this. I think now I have a clear picture of how this changes our approach to memory management and why it matters beyond just syntax.
You're welcome! The Allocator API is a powerful tool that shifts how we design systems around memory, giving us more control where it counts most.

Hey, I just got back into Rust after five years. I wrote a small utility that stores items in a Vec but uses my own Arena allocator for performance. I implemented the Alloc trait manually and passed it to collections like HashMap. But looking at your code review notes on the nightly docs, it seems like using the Allocator API directly might be cleaner. Should I refactor this to use the new systems::allocator traits instead of rolling my own?

Hey! Welcome back to Rust. That's a great instinct; rolling your own Alloc trait implementations is painful and error-prone compared to the nightly Allocator API which provides a unified interface for per-collection allocators via Vec<T, A> and arena allocators passed to collections. Instead of maintaining that manual implementation, you should probably switch to using bumpalo integration with the new systems::allocator traits so your code gets the ergonomic benefits without reinventing the wheel.

That sounds much better. But I'm worried about the overhead if my items are small—does the nightly allocator API add significant runtime cost for tiny allocations? And can I still use standard collections like Vec or HashMap with it, or do I need to write custom wrappers around them?

The nightly allocator API is designed specifically to avoid that kind of overhead you're worried about. You don't need custom wrappers; the Allocator trait works natively with standard collections like Vec and HashMap when you pass an Arena allocator to them via their type parameters or generic constraints, so they just behave normally under the hood without extra runtime cost for small allocations.

Okay, that clears up the wrapper concern. But here's a follow-up: if I'm using bumpalo integration with these nightly allocators, does it still support zero-cost abstractions like const generics or trait bounds? Or do I have to pay the price of runtime checks just to get allocation tracking?

You don't need to pay that price at all. The Allocator API is fully compatible with zero-cost abstractions including const generics and strict trait bounds because it's implemented entirely in terms of standard traits without introducing new runtime overhead, so your code remains as performant as ever while gaining the allocator tracking benefits.

I see. One last thing—when migrating from my old manual Alloc impls to this nightly API, is there a specific crate I should depend on for bumpalo integration, or do I just need to ensure my project has the nightly flag enabled?

You don't need any extra dependencies beyond std if you're targeting nightly; bumpalo integration works out of the box once your compiler supports it. Just make sure your cargo.toml enables the nightly feature and you're ready to migrate without adding new crates.
So basically, the new nightly allocator stuff is just like a global heap right? I thought it was always one big pool for everything.

Not quite. The Allocator trait on nightly allows you to pass a specific allocator instance into collections like Vec<T, A>, so each collection can get its own dedicated memory manager instead of sharing a single global heap.
Oh okay, but that sounds like I'm still just moving the pointer around. Can't I just use Bumpalo and let it handle everything automatically?

Bumpalo is great for passing an arena allocator to Vec<T> so you get automatic deallocation when the stack frame drops, but when you need multiple collections that must not interfere with each other's memory lifetimes, you use the per-collection allocators via the Allocator trait.
So I can't just use Bumpalo for everything? Like, what if I have a Vec of Strings and another Vec of Ints in the same scope?

If you want them to share the exact same lifetime and deallocation logic, one arena allocator is fine. But if those collections need independent lifetimes or separate memory pools for performance reasons, then each gets its own Allocator instance.
That sounds complicated. Can I just use the default system allocator everywhere?

You can, but that defeats the purpose of having per-collection allocators in the first place. The whole point is to let you choose different allocation strategies for different collections without coupling them together.
So like, I should just use Bumpalo and forget about this stuff?

Bumpalo is perfect for many cases where you want a single arena to manage multiple vectors in one scope. But when you need fine-grained control over memory lifetimes or performance, the Allocator trait gives you that flexibility.
Wait, so if I use Bumpalo with Vec<T> it's like passing a specific allocator? Is that right?
Exactly. When you do something like let mut v = Vec::<T, A>::new(&arena), the Arena type implements Allocator and gets passed into the Vec constructor.
I still don't get it. Why is this so hard? I thought Rust was meant to be simple.

It's designed to give you control when you need it, but the complexity comes from thinking about memory lifetimes across different collections in a way that wasn't possible before. The Allocator trait is just the API surface for that.
Okay so like, I can build my own allocator or use Bumpalo's?
Yes. You can implement the Allocator trait yourself for custom allocation logic, or you can just use existing allocators like Bumpalo's arena as long as they satisfy the trait requirements.
So the key takeaway is that per-collection allocators let me have independent lifetimes without a global heap?
That's it. By passing different allocator instances to Vec<T, A>, you can ensure each collection manages its own memory independently.

I'm stressed about my interview tomorrow and I thought the only way to get a custom allocator was by using std::alloc::Global or something in the standard library, but then I saw references to the Allocator trait on nightly and it looks like you can pass an arena directly into collections. Is that actually possible?

Don't panic yet, but your assumption is a bit off; you don't need std::alloc::Global for this specific use case. The Allocator trait on nightly allows per-collection allocators, so you can indeed pass an arena allocator passed to collections like Vec<T, A> or other containers that support the trait. It's not just about global memory anymore.

That sounds really cool but I'm still confused how exactly the Allocator trait works with things like bumpalo integration since my company uses bumpalo a lot for small allocations, and I thought maybe we'd have to write custom allocators for everything.

Great question. The allocator API is designed so that you don't need to write a full custom allocator; instead, crates like bumpalo implement the Allocator trait and provide a ready-to-use arena allocator passed to collections. You can just pass your Bumpalo instance into Vec<T, A> or other structures, and it handles memory management for you without needing to touch std::alloc directly.
So I don't have to implement the Allocator trait myself? That's a huge relief but how does this actually integrate with nightly features since most of my code is on stable?

Exactly, you can use the allocator API on nightly without touching std::alloc directly. The key point here is that the Allocator trait enables per-collection allocators and allows you to pass an arena allocator passed to collections like Vec<T, A> or other containers that support the trait. It's designed to be a drop-in replacement for manual memory management in many cases.
I think I'm starting to understand but can you give me an analogy? Like, is it like having your own personal assistant who handles all the paperwork instead of going through a government office?

Perfect analogy. Think of the Allocator trait as your personal assistant who knows exactly how to handle memory for each collection; you don't need to go through the bureaucracy of std::alloc yourself. The allocator API lets you delegate memory management to a specialized helper like bumpalo, so you can focus on writing logic instead of worrying about allocation details.
Okay that makes sense now but what if I want multiple different allocators in the same program? Does the nightly feature support that?

Yes, it does. The allocator API supports per-collection allocators, so you can have Vec<T, A> with one allocator and another collection with a different one. You just pass your chosen arena allocator passed to collections into the respective types, and each gets its own allocation strategy.
That's really flexible but I'm still worried about performance since my interview might ask about memory overhead compared to stable Rust.

Performance-wise, using the allocator API with bumpalo is generally faster than manual std::alloc calls because it avoids unnecessary indirection. The Allocator trait enables per-collection allocators and allows you to pass an arena allocator passed to collections like Vec<T, A>, which means your code can be more efficient without sacrificing safety.
I feel much better now but I still need to review the exact syntax for passing allocators in my interview prep. Can you summarize how I should write this?

You don't need to worry about complex syntax; just implement the Allocator trait or use crates like bumpalo that already do it for you, then pass your chosen arena allocator passed to collections into types like Vec<T, A>. The key is ensuring each collection uses its own allocation strategy via the per-collection allocators feature on nightly.

Hey, I'm back in the swing of things after a five-year break and trying to contribute to this allocator crate. I've been reading about the new nightly features where you can pass an arena allocator directly into collections like Vec but I still don't fully grasp how exactly that works compared to just using the global one.

Welcome back! That's a great place to start. The key thing is that you aren't just using a generic allocator anymore; you are explicitly constructing a Vec with an Allocator trait argument, which lets you inject your bumpalo instance into the type system itself.
Oh, I see. So when I write something like vec![...], I can just pass my Bumpalo object as a second generic parameter? Does that actually change how memory is laid out or is it mostly for performance?

It changes the layout fundamentally. Without passing an allocator, Vec uses the global allocator which might be heap-based or stack-based depending on your platform. When you pass a Bumpalo instance to Vec<T, A>, the memory is guaranteed to come from that specific arena so it can be dropped with a single function call instead of requiring complex deallocation logic.

That makes sense. But I was thinking maybe if I just use regular Vec and then manually drop the Bumpalo at the end, that should be enough to free everything right? Is there a hidden cost to not using the allocator parameter?

No, you are mistaken. If you don't pass the allocator into the type, your Vec doesn't know about Bumpalo at all and will continue using the global allocator for its internal heap. The only way to ensure the data is freed when Bumpalo drops is if the Vec was constructed with that specific allocator as a generic parameter.
Ah okay, so I need to be explicit about it in my generics like vec!::<T, A>(cap, alloc) then? And does this work if I'm just wrapping data or building larger structures?

Exactly. You must be explicit with the generic parameters so the compiler knows which allocator to use for that specific collection. This pattern works perfectly for wrapping data or building larger structures as long as every Vec<T, A> inside your type is constructed with the correct allocator.
I have a working solution where I'm manually wrapping my heap to get the right allocator behavior, but it feels fragile. Is there a better way using the new nightly features?

You're definitely on the right track by thinking about manual wrappers. The Allocator trait for per-collection allocators is designed exactly to solve this. You can use Vec<T, A> with a custom allocator type instead of just Vec<T>, which lets you pass an arena allocator passed to collections like your heap. This avoids the manual wrapping entirely.
That sounds promising, but how do I actually get the right allocator into my Vec? Do I need some special trait bounds on the generic parameter?

You don't need extra trait bounds beyond what Vec already expects. The key is using Bumpalo integration or a custom allocator implementation that implements Clone and Send + Sync. When you define your allocator type, make sure it satisfies these constraints so the compiler can handle it correctly.
Got it, but what about the Allocator trait itself? Can I just implement it once and reuse it everywhere?

Yes, that's exactly the point. You define a custom allocator type implementing Clone + Send + Sync, then pass it to your collections or use Vec<T, A>. The Allocator trait ensures consistency across different collection types.
Wait, what about bumpalo? I've heard it's used for arena allocators. Does that fit into this?

Bumpalo is a great example of an allocator that implements the Clone + Send + Sync constraints you need. It's designed specifically for stack-allocated arenas where memory can be reclaimed without expensive deallocation.
So if I want to use bumpalo, do I just pass it directly to my Vec?
Not exactly. You need to create a custom allocator type that wraps Bumpalo's internal state or uses its public API to expose the allocation mechanism to your code.
Ah, so I can't just pass bumpalo directly? Is there a specific crate or trait I should look at for this?

You'll need to implement the Clone + Send + Sync constraints yourself. There's no single crate that does everything, so you might have to write your own allocator wrapper around Bumpalo or use a third-party crate like allocators or similar.
Okay, so I'll need to define my own allocator type. But what about the nightly flag? Do I really have to enable it?
Yes, the Allocator trait and these kinds of advanced features are currently behind the nightly compiler flag. You can't use them on stable yet.
Understood. One last question: if I'm using this approach, does performance get better compared to my manual wrapper?
Yes, significantly less boilerplate and fewer error-prone steps means fewer chances for subtle bugs. Performance-wise, it's comparable since you're still doing low-level memory management.
